title: What is a Production Build?
intro: Your team just deployed a new feature, but the build failed on the server even though everything works locally. Think of it like packing a suitcase -- if something doesn't fit right, the whole trip is delayed. You need to figure out what went wrong and get the site back online.

teach.title: Building = Packing for a Trip
teach.body: A production build is like packing a suitcase for a trip. You don't bring your entire closet -- you pick only what you need, fold everything neatly, and compress it to fit. The **build process** takes your messy development code and creates a clean, compressed, optimized version that loads fast for users.

teach.title: Build = Compressing a Photo Before Texting
teach.body: When you take a photo, the raw file is massive -- great for editing but terrible for sending. Before texting it, your phone automatically compresses it so it sends fast without losing visible quality. A production build does the same to your code: it squeezes out all the extra stuff (comments, whitespace, unused code) so your app loads lightning-fast for users.

teach.title: What Happens During a Build
teach.body:
When you run `npm run build`, several things happen:

1. **Bundling** -- All your separate files get combined into fewer files
2. **Minification** -- Variable names get shortened, whitespace removed
3. **Tree-shaking** -- Unused code gets removed
4. **Compilation** -- TypeScript, JSX, and modern syntax get converted to plain JavaScript
5. **Optimization** -- Images compressed, CSS purged of unused styles

teach.title: Before and After
teach.annotation: The minified version does the exact same thing but is much smaller. This is why production builds load faster.

teach.body: A fast-loading app keeps users around long enough to become paying customers. Mastering production builds means your vibecoded app feels snappy and professional from the first visit.
A production build bundles, minifies, and optimizes your code so it loads fast for users. Build errors are one of the most common deployment problems.

start.narrative: You push to main and the deploy starts. Two minutes later, you get an alert: 'Build failed'. The site is still showing the old version. Your PM is asking when the new feature will be live. What do you do first?

choices.text: Open the build logs on the hosting platform
Push the same code again -- maybe it was a temporary glitch
Tell the PM it will be ready tomorrow and go to lunch

check-logs.narrative:
Good call! You open the build logs and see:

Error: Module not found: Can't resolve './components/Header'
  at ./src/pages/index.js

Build failed with 1 error.

The file exists on your computer. What could be wrong?

choices.text: Check if the filename casing matches -- Mac is case-insensitive but Linux servers aren't
Delete the node_modules folder and reinstall
The server must be broken -- contact hosting support

retry-blind.narrative: You push again and... same error. Build failures are almost never temporary glitches -- they're caused by real problems in your code or configuration. You need to actually read the error message.

choices.text: Okay, let me check the build logs

ignore.narrative: Ignoring a build failure means the new feature isn't live and you're no closer to fixing it. The PM will ask again after lunch. Better to investigate now while the error is fresh.

choices.text: Fine, let me check the build logs

found-issue.narrative: Bingo! Your file is named 'header.js' but your import says './components/Header'. On your Mac, this works because macOS is case-insensitive. The Linux build server is case-sensitive -- 'Header' and 'header' are different files. How do you fix it?

choices.text: Use 'git mv components/header.js components/Header.js' so Git records the casing change
Just rename the file in Finder and commit -- Git will pick it up
Change the import to lowercase: ./components/header

reproduce-locally.narrative: You fixed the file. Before you push to main and find out from the build server whether it works, how do you confirm the production build actually succeeds on your laptop?

choices.text: Run 'npm run build' locally and watch for errors before pushing
Push to a preview branch first and let the platform's preview deploy verify it
Push straight to main -- the local dev server already worked

check-bundle.narrative: The build succeeds. Now the deploy goes out, but the page is showing slowly on mobile. You open the build output and see the JS bundle is 4.2MB. What's the most useful next step?

choices.text: Run 'npx vite-bundle-visualizer' (or the equivalent for your bundler) to see which dependency is fattest
Enable gzip on the CDN and call it done -- compression will hide the size
Ignore the size -- 4.2MB is normal for a modern app

prevent-future.narrative: You shaved the bundle down by tree-shaking an oversized icon library. To stop the next 'works locally, fails on Linux' surprise, what do you put in CI?

choices.text: Add a CI step that runs 'npm run build' on Linux for every PR -- failed builds block the merge
Set up a pre-push Git hook that runs the build locally before allowing a push
Put a note in the README telling people to remember to test the build

end-good.narrative: You read the build logs, fixed the casing with 'git mv', verified the build locally, trimmed the bundle, and added CI that runs the production build on Linux for every PR. The next case-sensitivity bug -- and there will be one -- can never reach main.
end-good.feedback: This is what mature deployment hygiene looks like. The most important step was the CI build on Linux: it removes the gap between your laptop and the production environment, which is where 90% of 'works on my machine' bugs hide. Pair this with a bundle-size check and you've got a deploy pipeline that catches problems before users do.

end-okay.narrative: You shipped a clean fix and a pre-push hook. The hook helps for you, but a teammate on Windows or a contractor without your dotfiles won't have it -- so the bug class isn't fully closed.
end-okay.feedback: Solid recovery. Local hooks help individuals; CI helps the whole team. Move the build check to CI so every contributor gets the same safety net regardless of their machine setup.

end-weak.narrative: The site is back online but nothing structural changed. The next case-sensitivity bug, the next missing dependency, the next platform-specific surprise will all reach main again, because the only safeguard is people remembering to read the README.
end-weak.feedback: You fixed today's bug, but you didn't shrink tomorrow's bug surface. Documentation is a wish; CI is a guarantee. One 'npm run build' step in CI would have caught this without any human attention.
